concepts.title: Terms related to quantum numbers
concepts.conceptBody:
Quantum Numbers

To understand the concept of Quantum Numbers, we must know the meaning of some terms clearly so as to avoid any confusion.

Energy Level:

The non-radiating energy paths around the nucleus are called as Energy Levels or Shells. These are specified by numbers having values1,2,3,4, …. or $\text{K},\text{L},\text{M},\text{N},\ldots $ corresponding to $1,2,3,4,\ldots $ in order of increasing energies. The energy of a particular energy level is fixed.

Sub-Energy Level:

The phenomenon of splitting of spectral lines in electric and magnetic fields reveals that there must be extra energy levels with-in a definite energy level. These were called as Sub-Energy Levels or Sub-Shells. There are four types of sub-shells namely; s, p, d, f.

First energy level ( $K$ or 1 ) has on sub-shell designed as $1~\text{s}$, the second energy level ( $\text{L}$ or 2 ) has two sub-shells as 2s \& 2p, the third energy level (M or 3) has three sub-shells as 3s, 3p \& 3d, and the fourth energy level ( $\text{N}$ or 4 ) has four sub-shells s $4~\text{s},4\text{p},4~\text{d }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }4\text{f}$. The energy sub-shells increases roughly in the order: $\text{s}<\text{p}<\text{d}<\text{f}$.

Orbital:

Each sub-energy level (sub-shell) is composed of one or more orbitals. These orbitals belonging to a particular sub-shell have equal energies and are called as degenerate orbitals. Sub-shell s has one orbital, $p$ has three orbitals, $d$ has five orbitals and $f$ has seven orbitals.

To describe or to characterise the electrons around, the nucleus in an atom, a set of four numbers is used, called as Quantum Numbers. These are specified such that the states available to the electrons should follow the laws of quantum mechanics or wave mechanics.

concepts.title: Principle Quantum number
concepts.conceptBody:
Principal Quantum Number: (n)

This number represents the main energy levels (principal energy levels) designated as $\text{n}=1,2,3$,... or the corresponding shells are named as $\text{K},\text{L},\text{M},\text{N},\ldots $ respectively. It gives an idea of position and energy of an electron. The energy level $n=1$ corresponds to minimum energy and subsequently $n=2$, $3,4,\ldots $ are arranged in order of increasing energy.

  • Higher is the value of $\text{n}$, greater is its distance from the nucleus, greater is its size and also greater is its energy.
  • It also gives the total electrons that may be accommodated in each shell, the capacity of each shell is given by the formula $2{{\text{n}}^{2}}$, where $\text{n}$: principal quantum number.

concepts.title: Azimuthal Quantum number
concepts.conceptBody:
Azimuthal Quantum Number: (l)

This number determines the energy associated with the angular momentum of the electron about the nucleus. It is also called as the angular momentum quantum number. It accounts for the appearance of groups of closely packed spectral lines in electric field (Zeeman Lines).

  • It can assume all integral values from 0 to $n-1$. The possible values of 1 are: $1,2,3,\ldots ,\text{n}-1$
  • Each value of 1 describes a particular sub-shell with in the main energy level and determines the shape of the electron cloud.
  • When $n=1,l=0$, i.e., its energy level contains one sub-shell which is called as a sub-shell. So for $\text{l}=0$, the corresponding sub-shell is s sub-shell. Similarly when $\text{l}=1,2,3$, the sub-shells are called as $\text{p},\text{d},\text{f}$ sub-shells respectively.
  • As you know for $\text{n}=1,\text{l}=0$, there is only one sub-shell. It is represented by $1~\text{s}$. Now for $\text{n}=$ 2,1 can take two values (the total number of values taken by $l$ is equal to the value of $n$ in a particular energy level). The possible values of 1 are 0,1. The two sub-shells representing the IInd energy level are $2~\text{s},2\text{p}$. In the same manner, for $\text{n}=3$, three sub-shells are designated as $3~\text{s},3\text{p},3~\text{d}$ corresponding to $\text{l}=0,1,2$ and for $\text{n}=4$, four sub-shells are designated as $4~\text{s},4\text{p}$, $4~\text{d}4\text{f}$ corresponding to $\text{l}=0,1,2,3$.
  • The orbital Angular momentum of electron $\sqrt{1(1+1)} \frac{h}{2 \pi}$

Note that its value does not depend upon value of $n$.

concepts.title: Magnetic quantum number
concepts.conceptBody:
Magnetic Quantum Number: (m)

An electron with angular quantum number can be thought as an electric current circulating in a loop. A magnetic field due to this current is observed. This induced magnetism is determined by the magnetic quantum number. Under the influence of magnetic field, the electrons in a given sub-energy level prefer to orient themselves in certain specific regions in space around the nucleus. The number of possible orientations for a sub-energy level is determined by possible values of $\text{m}$ (this corresponds to the number of orbitals in a given sub-energy level).

  • $m$ can have any integral values between -1 to +1 including 0, i.e., $m=-1,-1+1,\ldots ...,0,1,2$, $3,\ldots ..,l-1,+1$. We can say that a total of $(2l+1)$ values of $m$ are there for a given value of 1.
  • In $\text{s}$ sub-shell, there is only one orbital $[1=0,\Rightarrow \text{m}=(2\text{l}+1)=1]$.
  • I p sub-shell, there are three orbitals corresponding to three values of $m:-1,0,+1$. $[\text{l}=1\Rightarrow \text{m}=(2\text{l}+1)=3]$. These three orbitals are represented as ${{\text{p}}_{\text{x}}},{{\text{p}}_{\text{y}}},{{\text{p}}_{\text{z}}}$ along $\text{X},\text{Y},\text{Z}$ axes perpendicular to each other.
  • In $\text{d}$ sub-shell, there are five orbitals corresponding to $-2,-1,0,+1,+2.[1=2\Rightarrow \text{m}=$ $(2\text{l}+1)=5]$. These five orbitals are represented as ${{d}_{xy}},{{d}_{yz}},{{d}_{zx}},{{d}_{x}}2-y,{{d}_{z}}2$.
  • In $\text{f}$ sub-shell there are seven orbitals corresponding to $-3,-2,-1,0,+1,+2,+3$. $[l=3\Rightarrow \text{m}=(2\text{l}+1)=7]$

concepts.title: Spin quantum number
concepts.conceptBody:
Spin Quantum Number: (s)

When an electron rotates around a nucleus it also spins about its axis. If the spin is clockwise, its spin quantum number is $(+1/2)$ and is represented as $\uparrow $ or 1. If the spin is anti-clockwise, its value is $-1/2$ and is represented as $\downarrow $ or 1. If the value of $\text{s}$ is $(+1/2)$ then by convention, we take that electron as the first electron in that orbital and if the value of $\text{s}$ is $(-1/2)$ it is taken as second electron.

concepts.title: shapes of atomic orbitals
concepts.conceptBody:
Shapes of Atomic Orbitals

 

The p-orbitals consist of two lobes with the atomic nucleus lying between them. The axis of each p-orbital is perpendicular to the other two. The ${{p}_{x}},{{p}_{y}}$ and ${{p}_{z}}$ orbitals are equivalent except for their directional property. They have the same energy. Orbitals having the same energy are said to be degenerate.

concepts.title: Quantum mechanical model
concepts.conceptBody:
Quantum Mechanical Model

In the quantum mechanical model of an atom, there is only a "chance" or probability of finding an electron in space. Where this probability is maximum (over 95\%), that region of space is called 'an orbital'.

The concept of orbital comes from the Schrodinger wave equation .

$\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \Psi}{\mathrm{dx}^2}+\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \Psi}{\mathrm{dy}^2}+\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \Psi}{\mathrm{dz}^2}+\frac{8 \pi^2 \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{~h}^2}(\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{V}) \psi=0$

The symbols have the following meanings:

$\text{m}\to $ Mass of an electron

$\text{h}\to $ Planck's constant

$\text{E}\to $ Total energy of electron

$\text{V}\to $ Potential energy of electron

$\Psi \to $ Wave function

Electrons have associated wave functions denoted by $\Psi $

$\Psi $ itself has no physical significance. Its square, however, gives the probability of finding an electron at a given point. More often, we are interested in finding the probability of finding an electron in a region. For this, consider a sphere of radius $r$ and thickness dr.

Since this function depends only on the value of radial distance from the centre, it is called radial probability function.

Radial node or Spherical nodes: Now, in any atom, the quantum number $\text{n}$ gives the distance between the nucleus \& the outermost shell. So, it is a measure of the radial distance of an electron. It so happens that for certain orbitals, the probability of finding an electron drops to zero at certain distances from the nucleus. Such points are called radial nodes. (A node is a region where the probability of finding an electron is zero).

Following graphs will further illustrate this point

The number of radical nodes increased with the increase in distance between nucleus and the outermost shell.

Angular or Planar node: This type of node corresponds to the quantum number l. In s orbitals, due to their spherical symmetry, there are no nodal plane. Consider ${{\text{p}}_{\text{x}}}$ orbital.

In the $\text{y}-\text{z}$ plane, the probability of finding an electron is $\text{O}$. So, $\text{y}-\text{z}$ is a nodal plane.
